name: Tutorial 3: USP Therapeutic Category Classification
description: Classifies Medicare Part D drugs into USP therapeutic categories using a reference table JOIN. Demonstrates how to add analytical structure to raw spending data — the foundation for GUARD payment model analysis.

narration.pre: Starting a classification workflow. The goal: take raw Part D drug spending data and classify each drug into a USP therapeutic category using a reference table JOIN. This transforms a flat spending list into a structured analytical dataset.
narration.post: Session initialized. Let's import the dataset and the USP category mapping.

narration.pre: Importing the CMS Medicare Part D Drug Spending dataset — the same 500-row sample used in Tutorial 1. This time we'll focus on classification rather than exclusion.
narration.post: Dataset loaded: 500 rows, 46 columns. Each row is a drug-manufacturer combination. We filter to Mftr_Name='Overall' rows for unique drug counts. Next: load the USP category reference table.
Insights generated. Now let's load the classification reference table.

narration.pre: Loading the USP GUARD Categories reference table. This maps 102 generic drug names to one of 17 USP therapeutic categories (e.g., Cardiovascular, Antineoplastics, Metabolic/Endocrine). The USP Model Guidelines for Medicare categorize drugs for the GUARD payment model.
narration.post: Reference table loaded: 102 drugs mapped to therapeutic categories. Columns: generic_name, usp_category, usp_category_code, notes. We'll JOIN this against the spending dataset on generic name to classify each drug.

details.sql: SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT Brnd_Name) AS total_drugs FROM dataset WHERE Mftr_Name = 'Overall'

narration.pre: First, establish the universe: how many unique drugs are in the Part D dataset? We filter to 'Overall' manufacturer rows to avoid counting the same drug multiple times across manufacturers.
narration.post: 272 unique drugs. This is our full universe — same as Tutorial 1. The classification question is: how many of these 272 can we map to a USP therapeutic category?

details.sql: SELECT Brnd_Name, Gnrc_Name, Tot_Spndng_2023 FROM dataset WHERE Mftr_Name = 'Overall' ORDER BY Tot_Spndng_2023 DESC LIMIT 10

narration.pre: Before classifying, let's see the top 10 spenders. Knowing which drugs dominate spending helps us understand whether our classification coverage matters — if the top spenders are classified, we capture most of the spending even if coverage is incomplete.
narration.post: Top 10 includes Eliquis, Ozempic, Humira, Keytruda, Revlimid. These span multiple therapeutic categories — cardiovascular, metabolic, immunologic, oncology. Good classification coverage of the top spenders is critical.

details.sql: SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT p.Brnd_Name) AS classified_count FROM dataset p INNER JOIN reference u ON p.Gnrc_Name = u.generic_name WHERE p.Mftr_Name = 'Overall'

narration.pre: Now the key classification query: how many of our 272 drugs match the USP reference table? The INNER JOIN matches on generic name — only drugs with an exact generic name match will be classified.
narration.post: 68 out of 272 drugs classified — about 25% coverage by drug count. The unclassified 204 drugs are the 'residual' that would need manual classification or an expanded reference table. But these 68 drugs likely account for a much larger share of total spending.

details.sql: SELECT u.usp_category, ROUND(SUM(p.Tot_Spndng_2023)) AS total_spending, COUNT(*) AS drug_count FROM dataset p INNER JOIN reference u ON p.Gnrc_Name = u.generic_name WHERE p.Mftr_Name = 'Overall' GROUP BY u.usp_category ORDER BY total_spending DESC

narration.pre: Now the analytical payoff: spending by USP therapeutic category. This is the query that transforms flat drug spending data into a structured view — exactly what the GUARD payment model needs for category-level analysis.
narration.post: 9 therapeutic categories represented. Metabolic/Endocrine leads (includes GLP-1 drugs like Ozempic), followed by Cardiovascular (Eliquis) and Antineoplastics (oncology drugs). This category-level view is the foundation for GUARD payment model rate-setting — each category gets its own spending benchmark.

details.sql: SELECT u.usp_category, p.Brnd_Name, p.Gnrc_Name, p.Tot_Spndng_2023 FROM dataset p INNER JOIN reference u ON p.Gnrc_Name = u.generic_name WHERE p.Mftr_Name = 'Overall' ORDER BY u.usp_category, p.Tot_Spndng_2023 DESC LIMIT 50

narration.pre: Finally, let's see the classified drugs listed by category with their spending. This is the detailed view that an analyst would export for policy review — each drug with its therapeutic classification and spending level.
narration.post: 50 classified drugs with their USP categories. This is the deliverable: a structured, categorized drug spending list ready for GUARD analysis. In a full workflow, you'd expand the reference table to classify the remaining 204 drugs, either by adding more entries or using the Generic Name Pattern Classifier (a planned AW feature).

narration.pre: Exporting the classified drug list for policy review.
narration.post: Export complete. The Excel file contains drugs categorized by USP therapeutic class, ready for GUARD payment model analysis.

Session summary: Classified 68 of 272 Part D drugs into 9 USP therapeutic categories via reference table JOIN. Metabolic/Endocrine and Cardiovascular categories dominate spending. The classification workflow — import, reference JOIN, GROUP BY category — is the foundation for GUARD payment model rate-setting. Next step: expand the reference table to improve coverage beyond 25%.

resume_state.last_sql: SELECT u.usp_category, p.Brnd_Name, p.Gnrc_Name, p.Tot_Spndng_2023 FROM dataset p INNER JOIN reference u ON p.Gnrc_Name = u.generic_name WHERE p.Mftr_Name = 'Overall' ORDER BY u.usp_category, p.Tot_Spndng_2023 DESC LIMIT 50
